entry.title: Re: Can we reassign job requisitions automatically in SAP...
entry.content:
@Sanjay789 wrote:

Can we reassign job requisitions automatically in SAP SF? If yes, how ? I know we can transfer requisition into other's name manually.

Hello @Sanjay789,
Yes, you can automatically reassign job requisitions in SAP SuccessFactors using Integration Center. Here's how:

  1. Navigate to Integration Center: Go to Admin Center > Integration Center.

  2. Create Integration: Select My Integrations > Create > More Integration Types. 3. Configure Trigger: Choose Trigger Type as Scheduled. Set Source and Destination as SuccessFactors. 4. Map Fields: Map the fields from the source job requisition to the destination job requisition. Ensure you include all necessary details.

For detailed instructions, refer to the SAP SuccessFactors documentation.

Does this help? Let me know if you need any further assistance!


Best regards,
James Goff

entry.title: Re: Bank Integration through API
entry.content:
@forumuser wrote:

Dear Team,

We are in process of API based Bank Integration where the data will be sent in a JASON file using CPI. We are not clear on:

1. What config we need to do in our system?

2. What program we need to maintain under FBZP> Payment method in Country > Use Classic payment medium programs (RFFO*)

Any other thing we need to consider?

Thanks in advance

Hello @forumuser,
For your API-based bank integration using CPI (Cloud Platform Integration), here’s what you need to do:

  1. System Configuration: Ensure your system is correctly configured to send JSON files. Set up communication arrangements and define endpoints in SAP CPI.

  2. Maintain Program in FBZP: Under FBZP > Payment Methods in Country, choose the classic payment medium programs (RFFO*) applicable to your setup. You might need RFFOUS_T for US payments or RFFOD__S for SEPA payments.

Additionally, consider the following:

  • API Endpoints: Make sure your API endpoints are secure and well-documented.

  • Authentication: Implement proper authentication mechanisms like OAuth2 to protect your data transfer.

You can refer to the for detailed guidance.

Thanks in advance!


Best regards,
Jennifer Block

entry.title: Re: 关于生产核算3F0的科目如何在财务报表中配置
entry.content:
Dear,

这几个科目在损益表中没有位置存在

损益表包括：收入，成本，费用，利息，投资，资产减值，营业外等报表项目。

而这几个科目可以理解为成本核算的暂时技术科目，和其他科目：生产成本-差异 汇总在一起发生额是零

但是需要保证 生产类的成本中心余额为零，实际成本差异结转和差异科目为零。

这样资产负债表中本期间的未分配利润减去前一期间的未分配利润 等于本期间的利润表的净利润。

Regards,

Sen

entry.title: Re: MDG Cloud Ready Mode: Custom Workflow template in SAP build process automation with S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi Mustafa,

Yes, you are right it's not embedded at this moment and users need to navigate to MDG to review the details. If you see MDG cloud edition which is the SAAS version of MDG, the task UI shows all the details related to the business partner. But to the best of my knowledge, it can't be edited there, you need to go to MDG to edit it.

Coming to the benefits, Cloud ready mode is the steppingstone for the cloud first approach of SAP, which includes integration with Joule in 2023 FPS02. The MDG UI is native FIORI instead of web Dynpro, the validations can be done through MDG DQM instead of BADI. From a workflow perspective, building a workflow is easier in build process automation through drag and drop approach instead of coding heavy and BRF+ approach which are very technical.

Having said all this, we have to be cognizant of the fact that classic MDG has been around for more than 15 years and has evolved a lot during this time. Cloud ready mode now is more of an MVP product which is currently being aggressively worked upon by SAP.

Hope this helps, if you have any questions please do post it, for the benefits of the community.

Regards

Anirban

entry.title: Re: Create 2 goal sections on PM Form from samegoal plan,...
entry.content:
Hi Ellen

This is possible. We have a PM form with the same configuration.

Add the following code in bold letters to your xml in objective section 1 of the PM form.

 


<obj-sect-type autosync="true"><![CDATA[plan]]></obj-sect-type>
      <obj-sect-plan-id>#</obj-sect-plan-id>
      <obj-category><![CDATA[Name of category 1]]></obj-category>
      <meta-grp-label><![CDATA[Group]]></meta-grp-label>
    </objective-sect>
 
I think it used to work only when the code is added in one section. (In the meantime it might also work when you add the code in both sections.) If you add the code in one section, the goals of the other goal plan category are automatically shown in the other PM Form section.
 
Have a good day!
Andrea

entry.title: Master Data and Configuration Relationships in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I am working on setting up a new company configuration in SAP S/4HANA (version 2021). I want to understand how key master data elements and organizational units are related and what dependencies exist when creating them. Here's a list of the main elements I'm focusing on, along with their relevant database tables:

T000 - Client
T001 - Company Code
TKA01 - Controlling Area
TGSB - Business Area
CEPC - Profit Center
CSKS - Cost Center
PRPS - WBS
T001W - Plant
T001L - Storage Location
T024 - Purchasing Organization
T024E - Purchasing Group
BP001 - Business Partner
How are these entities related hierarchically, and what dependencies should I consider when configuring them?
EX, I know that when creating a Profit Center, it requires a Controlling Area. Similarly, creating a Storage Location requires a Plant.
Are there other dependencies like these I should be aware of?
Can someone provide a text-based ERD (Entity Relationship Diagram) or an example that maps out these relationships in a clear hierarchy?
Note:
If you have suggestions for adding other key master data to this list, please let me know.
Thanks in advance for your guidance!

entry.title: Developing CAP java/node.js mixed mono Multitenant SaaS Applications with Shared Database
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post, we will guide you through the process of creating a multitenant SaaS application with a shared database architecture, combining CAP Java and Node.js projects into a mono repository.

Step 1: Initialize the Project

1. Initialize the main project:

 

cds init onboarding 
rm -rf app srv​​

 

2. Initialize sub-module projects within the main project:

 

cds init node_project 
cds init java_project --java​​

 

3. Set up the workspace:

 

npm init -w ./node_project -w ./java_project

 

Step 2: Configure Multitenancy and HANA:

1. add multitenancy & hana

2. inside onboarding/mtx/sidecar/package.json, add build command in scripts.

 

"scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "build": "cds build ../.. --for mtx-sidecar --ws --production && npm ci --prefix gen"
}

 

3. Create a .cdsrc.json file at the root of the onboarding project:

 

"build": {
    "target": "."
  }
}

 

4.inside node_project/mtx/sidecar/package.json and java_project/mtx/sidecar/package.json, disable cds.xt.SaasProvisioningService:

 

"cds": {
    "requires": {
      "multitenancy": true,
      "extensibility": false,
      "toggles": false,
      "cds.xt.SaasProvisioningService": false,
      "db": {
        "kind": "hana-mt",
        "deploy-format": "hdbtable"
      }
}

 

5. Change the port of java_project/mtx/sidecar for local testing, e.g. 4006. Also change it in application.yaml.

 

"server": { 
      "port": 4006
}
cds:
  multi-tenancy:
    sidecar:
      url: http://localhost:4006/

 

6. Execute the build command in onboarding/mtx/sidecar. This will compose all DB artifacts from each workspace into the obboarding/db folder:

 

npm run build

 

Step 3: Deploy Database Artifacts

You can deploy the whole db artifacts with/without multitenancy.

1. For single tenant deployment:

 

cds build --ws --production
cds deploy --to hana:${hdi-name} --no-build

 

2. For multi-tenant deployment:

 

cds watch mtx/sidecar 
cds subscribe ${tenant_id} --to http://localhost:4005 -u name:password

 

Step 4: Start the Projects

1. Start java_project and java_project-mtx:

 

# run java mtx first 
cd java_project/mtx/sidecar 
npm run build 
cds watch 

# run java second 
cd java_project 
mvn spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=default

 

2. Start node_project and (using onboarding-mtx or start node_project-mtx):

 

# start node_project-mtx(optional) 
cd node_project 
cds build --production 
cds watch mtx/sidecar 

# start node_project 
cd node_project 
cds watch

 

Step 5: Local Testing

You can now test both the Node.js and Java applications locally.

Step 6: Add MTA Configuration and Deploy to BTP

1.Add the build:cf command to the package.json file of each workspace:

 

"build:cf": "cds build --production"

 

2. Create an mta.yaml file with the following content:

 

ID: com.sap.onboarding-mtxs
_schema-version: 3.3.0
version: 0.0.1
description: "Onboarding Service"

build-parameters:
  before-all:
    - builder: custom
      commands:
        - npm ci
        - npm run build:cf --workspace=node_project
        - npm run build:cf --workspace=java_project
modules:
  - name: node-mtxs-srv
    type: nodejs
    path: node_project/gen/srv
    parameters:
      buildpack: nodejs_buildpack
      readiness-health-check-type: http
      readiness-health-check-http-endpoint: /health
    properties:
      DEBUG: all
    build-parameters:
      builder: npm
    provides:
      - name: srv_api # required by consumers of CAP services (e.g. approuter)
        properties:
          url: ${default-url}
    requires:
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-service-manager
      - name: node-model-provider
        properties:
          CDS_MODEL_PROVIDER_URL: ~{url}
          CDS_MULTITENANCY_SIDECAR_URL: ~{url}
      - name: provisioning-service
        properties:
          CDS_MULTITENANCY_PROVISIONING_URL: ~{url}
          CDS_MULTITENANCY_PROVISIONING_POLLINGINTERVAL: 5s
          CDS_MULTITENANCY_PROVISIONING_POLLINGTIMEOUT: 5m
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-uaa
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-saas-registry

  - name: java-mtxs-srv
    type: java
    path: java_project/srv
    parameters:
      memory: 1024M
      disk-quota: 1024M
      buildpack: sap_java_buildpack
    properties:
      DEBUG: all
      JBP_CONFIG_COMPONENTS: "jres: ['com.sap.xs.java.buildpack.jdk.SAPMachineJDK']"
      JBP_CONFIG_SAP_MACHINE_JDK: '{ use_offline_repository: false, version: 21.+ }'
      JBP_CONFIG_JAVA_OPTS: "[java_opts: '-agentlib:jdwp=transport=dt_socket,address=8000,server=y,suspend=n,onjcmd=y']"
      SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE: cloud
      CDS_MULTITENANCY_MTXS_ENABLED: true
    build-parameters:
      ignore: [ "*.md" ]
      build-result: target/*.jar
    provides:
      - name: srv_java_api
        properties:
          url: '${default-url}'
    requires:
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-service-manager
      - name: java-model-provider
        properties:
          CDS_MODEL_PROVIDER_URL: ~{url}
          # CDS_MULTITENANCY_SIDECAR_URL: ~{url}
      - name: provisioning-service
        properties:
          CDS_MULTITENANCY_PROVISIONING_URL: ~{url}
          CDS_MULTITENANCY_PROVISIONING_POLLINGINTERVAL: 5s
          CDS_MULTITENANCY_PROVISIONING_POLLINGTIMEOUT: 5m
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-uaa

  - name: onboarding-mtxs-mtx
    type: nodejs
    path: mtx/sidecar
    parameters:
      memory: 512M
      disk-quota: 512M
    build-parameters:
      builder: custom
      build-result: gen
      commands:
        - npm run build
    properties:
      DEBUG: all
    requires:
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-uaa
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-service-manager
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-saas-registry
    provides:
      - name: provisioning-service
        properties:
          url: ${default-url}

  - name: onboarding-mtxs-node-model-provider
    type: nodejs
    path: node_project/gen/mtx/sidecar
    parameters:
      memory: 512M
      disk-quota: 512M
    build-parameters:
      builder: npm-ci
      # builder: custom
      # build-result: gen
      # commands:
      #   - npm run build
    properties:
      DEBUG: all
    requires:
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-uaa
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-service-manager
    provides:
      - name: node-model-provider
        properties:
          url: ${default-url}

  - name: onboarding-mtxs-java-model-provider
    type: nodejs
    path: java_project/mtx/sidecar
    parameters:
      memory: 512M
      disk-quota: 512M
    build-parameters:
      builder: custom
      build-result: gen
      commands:
        - npm run build
    properties:
      DEBUG: all
    requires:
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-uaa
      - name: onboarding-mtxs-service-manager
    provides:
      - name: java-model-provider
        properties:
          url: ${default-url}

resources:
  - name: onboarding-mtxs-service-manager
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: service-manager
      service-plan: container

  - name: onboarding-mtxs-uaa
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: xsuaa
      service-plan: broker
      config:
        xsappname: onboarding-mtxs-${org}-${space}
        tenant-mode: shared
        scopes:
          - name: $XSAPPNAME.mtcallback
            description: Multi Tenancy Callback Access
            grant-as-authority-to-apps:
              - $XSAPPNAME(application,sap-provisioning,tenant-onboarding)
          - name: $XSAPPNAME.mtdeployment
          - name: $XSAPPNAME.ExtendCDS
            description: Extend tenant models
          - name: $XSAPPNAME.ExtendCDSdelete
            description: Undeploy extensions
        authorities:
          - $XSAPPNAME.mtcallback
          - $XSAPPNAME.mtdeployment
        role-templates:
          - name: admin
            description: Admin
            scope-references:
              - $XSAPPNAME.mtcallback
              - $XSAPPNAME.mtdeployment
          - name: ExtensionDeveloper
            scope-references:
              - $XSAPPNAME.ExtendCDS
              - uaa.user
            description: CDS Extension Developer
          - name: ExtensionDeveloperUndeploy
            scope-references:
              - $XSAPPNAME.ExtendCDSdelete
              - $XSAPPNAME.ExtendCDS
              - uaa.user
            description: CDS Extension Developer with DB extension undeploy privilege (potential data loss!)              -
  #   generate a user and password for dynamic hdi deployer
  - name: onboarding-mtxs-saas-registry
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: saas-registry
      service-plan: application
      config:
        appName: onboarding-mtxs-${org}-${space} # this is the text on the tile
        xsappname: onboarding-mtxs-${org}-${space} # this is exactly the value from xsuaa.parameters.config.xsappname
        appUrls:
          # getDependencies: ~{srv_api/url}/mt/v1.0/subscriptions/dependencies
          # onSubscription: ~{srv_api/url}/mt/v1.0/subscriptions/tenants/{tenantId}
          getDependencies: ~{provisioning-service/url}/-/cds/saas-provisioning/dependencies
          onSubscription: ~{provisioning-service/url}/-/cds/saas-provisioning/tenant/{tenantId}
          onSubscriptionAsync: true
          onUnSubscriptionAsync: true
          onUpdateSubscriptionParametersAsync: true
          callbackTimeoutMillis: 300000
    requires:
      - name: provisioning-service

 

3. mbt build & cf deploy app.

4. Subscribe to this SaaS application in the Service Marketplace on BTP.

By following these steps, you should be able to successfully create and deploy a multitenant SaaS application with a mixed CAP Java/Node.js mono repository and a shared database architecture. If you encounter any issues or have any questions, please leave a comment below. I hope you find this guide helpful!

entry.title: Re: Enabling Access to SAP UI5 Applications for Non-BTP U...
entry.content:
Hi Khaing,

yes, this is absolutely possible, as SAP BTP differentiates between platform users and application users. Therefore, you can have trust configurations to identity providers for both of those user types within your BTP subaccount. By default, after setting up a subaccount, it will only have a trust configuration with the SAP default IDP, which uses SAP S-Users for authentication. As soon as you have set up a trust configuration to an IDP for application users, e.g. a SAP IAS tenant, and activated it for user logon, it can be used to access your custom application.

entry.title: New Modular Framework for Business Transformation Across All Functions
entry.content:
How new modular process framework can be used across all functions to do custom supplier lifecycle processes, either it can be supplier qualification/pre-qualification or miscellaneous process?

New modular process framework provide a flexible, repeatable framework for creating different supplier management processes for different types of suppliers (Domestic/Local, Overseas) using different combinations (On basis of Region, Commodity & Department/Business unit) of modular supplier management questionnaires.

Process projects allow you to tailor questionnaire content to specific suppliers, to leverage and reuse modular questionnaires across different processes, and to create custom processes for your specific supplier lifecycle activities.

Modular questionnaires for both qualification/prequalification and other process data collection activities.

Unlock creative configuration across all lifecycles by leveraging a process framework to streamline data collection. Address inefficiencies through diverse process types, both internal and external questionnaires, and reuse of existing questionnaires.

This feature is immediately available for all customers with the relevant solutions, though it requires completion of customer enablement steps.

Business Use Case - Buyer want to perform the Pre-Qualification for ACME supplier based on commodity, region and department matrix.

Based on requirement, SLP currently has a fixed set of lifecycle processes (registration, qualification, preferred), each with a single configurable template. Lifecycle processes could vary based on process type, and additionally by various commodity, region and department distinctions.

Current lifecycles result in a binary yes/no result, but data collection and qualification processes might  require a more nuanced final state.

Lastly, data collected in one questionnaire cannot be reused in another process, resulting in suppliers
potentially having to provide data multiple times.

Through New Modular Process Framework, We can collect the data for ACME supplier and reused it in another process based on Matrix (Region, Commodity & Department/Business unit).

Thanks! 

Please comment with the business use case which can not be achieved after reading the above post.

entry.title: Re: PULL request to deploy Github tested commits to SAP production system using gCTS
entry.content:
Hi Sunil,

we don't offer any option to create or execute Pull requests (or any other kind of workflow) inside the gCTS-App.
Comparing Commits or branches is possible.
For comparing commits, you can find the details in here https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/4a368c163b08418890a406d413933ba7/d27b12e8db0f453481adcb983a872063.html and for comparing branches in here https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/4a368c163b08418890a406d413933ba7/41623a50549c4fa3a55bef2997f4d870.html 

Also merging branches can be done in the gCTSApp: https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/4a368c163b08418890a406d413933ba7/de66bedd91ba469dab6f28dc60abcb05.html 

If conflicts occur during the merge, and gCTS can't automatically resolve the conflict, an error message is displayed. To continue the merge, you must resolve the conflicts.

Kind regards

Karin

entry.title: Inquiry regarding multiplex storage configuration
entry.content:
The customer is planning to migrate from a Unix environment to a Linux environment.

In a Linux environment, the customer wants to use the following CFS (Clustered File System) for sharing on SAN storage in an IQ Multiplex configuration. I am not sure if there are any restrictions on how to share storage in the manual, so I am asking for confirmation.

The CFS solution is Veritas infoscale Storage.

[SAP IQ Documentation Contents]

Any storage type can be used for SAP IQ as long as it is configured in a way that SAP IQ supports (raw devices on non-Linux platforms, local file systems for databases on all platforms, NFS on Linux, GPFS on Linux) and follows the appropriate sizing guidelines.

entry.title: Video Data Story, a brand-new way of story telling in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
The new feature "Video Data Story" will be introduced for SAP Analytics Cloud customers, since QRC1 2025. This feature will allow business users to design and create a short-form video directly inside SAP Analytics Cloud without any video editing expertise. It provides an alternative way to present the business insights using a brand-new format which could be more attractive and intuitive compared with the static content in story.

For how a Video Data Story looks like, please check the example video via:

https://video.sap.com/media/t/1_bu06re47

 

This blog will provide a preview of how Video Data Story looks like and how to create a Video Data Story.

- Enable "Video Data Story" for the related story

A new toggle is introduced in the story detail to enable the feature for related story. Please notice that Video Data Story is only supported for story designed with Optimized Story Experience.

- Create a Video Data Story 

In the story opened with view mode, a new option can be found via the context menu of each chart, which allows the user to add the selected chart into a new Video Data Story.

In the first release, the following chart types are supported to be added into Video Data Story:

Bar/Column chart, Line chart, Pie chart, Numeric Point chart.

Besides the above 4 chart types, the Text widget is also supported.

Once a chart is added, a Video Data Story design panel will be displayed in the bottom of the screen for the overview of the current Video Data Story with further configurations. 

In the design panel, the selected chart will be displayed as a tile with a preview of the animation effect so user will have the impression how this chart will be shown in the final video.

Repeat the above step to add more widgets into Video Data Story, and user can use drag and drop to change the sequence of selected widgets.

- Additional configuration for a Video Data Story

Besides adding a chart and changing the sequence, user can make some further configuration to make the final video more informative and attractive with personalized style.

  • Update the title and description to a chart

The updated title and description will also be shown in the preview of related tile.

  • Orientation

Portrait and Landscape are supported for the orientation of Video Data Story to fit with different scenarios when the Video Data Story is consumed.

  • Opening Title

User can add a title to the Video Data Story as the starting section.

  • Background Image

User can add a background image with light/dark theme from a pre-delivered lists of pictures.

If needed, users can also use a custom image by uploading the picture from local device.

  • Background Music

Similar with background image, user can also add music to the Video Data Story by selecting one music clip from a pre-delivered list.

  • Transition effect between charts

For two adjacent charts, if the data is related to each other, a special transition effect can be added to highlight the relationship in the final video. For example, user can first add a bar chart into the Video Data Story, and in the same chart, user can drill down or rank the chart, and add the same chart with updated status into Video Data Story again. In this case, user can decide if a Smart Transition effect need to be added for these two charts.

- Save and manage the Video Data Story

Once user is satisfied with the designed Video Data Story, he/she can save the Video Data Story.

The Video Data Story will be saved as a new file in SAP Analytics Cloud, which can be found and managed via the File Repository. This means, the saved Video Data Story can also be shared with other users or teams in SAP Analytics Cloud.

- View and Download the Video Data Story

The Video Data Story can be opened with View mode once it's saved. The video will be displayed in the browser directly.

User can further download the video to local device if needed. Once trigger the download, user will get a notification once the download file is ready, and the video will be downloaded as a WEBM file.

 

Above is a simple introduction about this new innovation in SAP Analytics Cloud, and of course, there will be further improvements and enhancements for Video Data Story in the future release.

entry.title: Data Archiving and Audit Log Management in SAP Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
In regulated industries such as pharmaceuticals and medical device manufacturing, data archiving and audit log management are not just operational necessities but are rather mandated by stringent regulatory frameworks. For example, FDA 21 CFR Part 11 and EU GMP Annex 11 require secure, reliable systems to ensure the integrity, availability, and long-term retention of electronic records and audit trails. These records must remain accessible for regulatory inspections and audits, often for retention periods, spanning from 5 to 10 or more years, depending on local laws and product lifecycles.

Data archiving involves securely storing historical data in a manner that prevents unauthorized changes while ensuring traceability and recoverability. For GxP-compliant organizations, this is critical to maintaining product quality, patient safety, and compliance with regulatory authorities.

In SAP Digital Manufacturing, we recognize the need for robust data archiving and audit log solutions to support customers in managing their compliance, storage, and data analysis requirements.

In this blog, we’ll explore how organizations can navigate data retention challenges in SAP Digital Manufacturing. By understanding the system’s current capabilities and aligning them with compliance strategies, customers improve adherence to regulatory standards, reduce compliance risks, and optimize their data management processes.

Data Archiving in SAP Digital Manufacturing

At present, SAP Digital Manufacturing does not offer a dedicated data archiving capability. However, it is important to note that all production data is securely stored in SAP Hana Cloud, SAP HANA database for persistent data storage, with a dedicated SAP Hana Cloud instance per tenant. Additional information related to HANA storage service entitlements can be found on SAP for Me. In addition, SAP Digital Manufacturing relies on the Object Store service within the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) to persist files. The Object Store service isolates data per tenant. Files are scanned for malware and viruses when they're uploaded. For more information on the Object Store service, please refer to What is Object Store?. In scenarios where storage constraints arise, SAP provides accommodations to ensure operations are not disrupted.

Furthermore, SAP Digital Manufacturing can integrate with SAP ERP or SAP S/4HANA systems for transactional data transfers. Manufacturing orders, for instance, can be archived in ERP systems, though this functionality is yet to be extended directly to SAP Digital Manufacturing. A full list of supported transactional data transfer processes can be found in SAP Help Portal.

Audit Log Retention and Viewer Capabilities

To meet regulatory compliance and operational transparency requirements, SAP Digital Manufacturing leverages the SAP BTP Audit Log Service. Audit logs are a cornerstone of compliance, providing a secure record of user activities such as who performed an operation, what was performed, and when. The current SAP Digital Manufacturing license includes a basic version of the SAP BTP Audit Log Service. By default, the logs are retained for free for 90 days. This provides logging capabilities for short-term compliance and tracking. Customers can explore the full scope of objects captured in these logs by referring to Security Guide for SAP Digital Manufacturing.

Customers can access the audit logs via two different channels. First is through the SAP BTP Audit Log Viewer, which is an intuitive UI application available at the subaccount level. While the BTP Audit Log Viewer is user-friendly for log management, it has a limitation of previewing only the first 500 records. For customers requiring access to larger datasets, customers can choose the Retrieval API, which is the 2nd way of accessing the audit logs. This API offers capabilities to retrieve and analyze audit logs at scale, making it an ideal choice for organizations with significant compliance or audit requirements. For more details, please visit the Retrieval API on SAP BTP Help Portal. 

For customers requiring extended retention of audit logs, the Premium Edition of the SAP BTP Audit Log Service is available. This commercialized, consumption-based plan allows logs to be retained for the duration of the subscription, supporting long-term storage requirements as mandated by regulations such as FDA 21 CFR Part 11 or EU GMP Annex 11, where retention periods can extend for several years. Customers can find information on the topic of audit log retention. Audit log entries are extremely light weight (typically under 0.02KB per entry), making this service a cost-effective solution for handling large volumes of logs. Learn more about the Premium Edition and its features on SAP BTP Help Portal. For pricing details and to calculate costs based on expected volumes, customers can visit SAP Discovery Center.

Conclusion

While SAP Digital Manufacturing doesn't offer built-in data archival capabilities, all production data is securely stored in SAP's database systems and remains there unless manually modified by users. This ensures that customers do not need to worry about data security or compliance in the absence of a dedicated archiving feature. SAP's Product Management team has plans to develop a dedicated archival service in future releases. For audit logs, customers have the option to extend the 90-day retention period to an unlimited premium service. This premium audit log version has consumption-based pricing, which scales with system usage. SAP Digital Manufacturing customers in regulated industries are strongly recommended to subscribe to this premium feature to ensure compliance.

SAP Digital Manufacturing

entry.title: How to understand the result list of Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation
entry.content:
Introduction

To get a first glimpse, the SAP Help Portal provides an overview and an explanation of the columns used in the result list of Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation: SAP Help Portal

This overview can be found for the other Advanced Valuation Runs (Discounting of Long-Term Assets and Liabilities, Credit-Risk-Based Impairment and B/S Reclassification) in the SAP Help Portal as well.

 

Different presentation of open items and non-open items in the result list

Entries in the result list referring to an open item can be identified with information in the columns: Original Journal Entry, Original Fiscal Year an Original Journal Entry Item. For open items, these columns carry information. This means every valuation journal entry has a direct and explicit connection to the original journal entry. This is a valuable information for further reporting purposes.

For entries in the result list referring to non-open items, the columns mentioned above don’t contain information. The reason behind is, that the line items are aggregated during the data selection.

 

User stories covered by the result list

In this chapter, several user stories with different intensions are defined. For every user story, the way how to read and understand the result list and get the information wanted is provided.  

Detailed view at the posted valuation journal entires

Comparison between posted journal entries (e.g. with the app Display Journal Entries in General Ledger) and entries of the result list. To start the comparison, the result list needs to be filtered for the status detail ‘POSTED’. After that, an FXV journal entry for further tracking purposes can be picked.

In this example, an exchange rate change between the current and prior Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation run was detected and the FXV Journal Entry 100003396 was created. The posted difference for this period is 90.00 USD.

This FXV Journal Entry can be found via the app Display Journal Entries in General Ledger, s. following screenshot:

Detection of an exchange rate difference

Check if an exchange rate difference between two execution of Advanced Foreign Currency runs was detected and posted (open items only). In case no exchange rate difference was detected, the column will carry the abbreviation ZERDEL, which stands for zero delta.


In case of an ERROR, of course the calculated difference will not be posted. This erroneous item will not have an impact on the whole run. Only this journal entry will not be created. After the fixing the issue, the item will be valuated and posted in the upcoming run (restart capability of the Advanced Valuation Runs).

Detection of erroneous entries in the result list

If an error occurs and no valuation can be posted, the status column carries the information ERROR. Possible root can be for example:  a missing business partner, fiscal period not open for posting, cost center locked, interest rates not properly maintained (Discounting of Long-Term Assets and Liabilities and/or Credit-Risk-Based Impairment), etc.

Analysis of erroneous documents

All erroneous items are carrying the status ERROR. The errors are listed in the application log and can be used for further analysis and correction purposes. Basically, the error messages provide a reference to the Group ID. Unfortunately, this information cannot be shown in the spool of the application. SAP is working on an improvement, to ensure this connection. This will improve the analysis capabilities.

Display of the valuation journal entry in a more detailed view

Since the Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation run uses the delta posting logic, this information cannot be extracted by the result list of a single valuation run. For this use case, a dedicated reporting capability need ot be used, described in the following KBA: KBA 3473536

Resulst list and currency type

As it can be seen in the following screenshot, per currency type one result list will be created.

The results (FX effects) for Functional Currency and Non-functional Currency are different if the currency types are different.

entry.title: Sell from Subcontractor - With Shipment Processing Part 2
entry.content:
Business Background

The sell-from-subcontractor cycle is a strategic solution that can be employed by companies to address manufacturing capacity constraints while ensuring efficient fulfilment of customer demands. In this process, raw materials are sent to a subcontractor, who leverages their facilities and expertise to manufacture finished goods in accordance with the company’s specifications. These goods are then maintained in customer consignment stock under the supplier’s ownership, providing flexibility and readiness for customer orders. When a customer demand arises, the company strategically authorizes the subcontractor to ship the finished products directly to the customer, bypassing internal logistics and minimizing lead times. This streamlined approach not only alleviates manufacturing bottlenecks but also enhances operational efficiency, reduces costs, and ensures prompt delivery. Leveraging SAP functionalities, such as subcontracting purchase orders, consignment stock management, and delivery processes, this cycle provides end-to-end visibility and control, enabling companies to maintain high levels of customer satisfaction and operational excellence. Below is the value chain diagram of the scenario.

Table of Content

  1. Customer Consignment Fill-up & Subcontracting Outbound Delivery Schedule Lines
  2. Requirement Class
  3. Sales Order Cost Estimate
  4. Customer Consignment Fill-up Pricing Procedure
  5. Subcontracting Delivery
  6. Route Determination for Outbound Delivery - Raw Materials
  7. Goods Issue on Shipment Completion
  8. Split Valuation
  9. Stock Determination

Visit https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/sell-from-subcontractor-with-shipment-processing-part-1/ba-p/13978299 for the Master Data & Business Processes Guide.

Configuration

The following configuration steps will be discussed to ensure the seamless execution of the sell-from-subcontractor process in SAP. First, the schedule line category for customer consignment will be configured to enable efficient planning and fulfilment of consignment stock. Next, the schedule line category for subcontracting outbound deliveries will be addressed to facilitate smooth delivery processes. Following this, the requirement class will be set up to define the necessary planning and procurement strategies. The configuration of the sales order cost estimate will then be covered to ensure accurate costing in the customer consignment fill-up process. Additionally, we will discuss the setup for the shipment of raw materials to the subcontractor, including movement types and handling requirements ensuring a fully integrated and traceable workflow across procurement and delivery stages. Finally, the configuration for split valuation and stock determination will be reviewed to enable precise inventory management and ensure optimal stock handling throughout the process. Note, New sales order type, delivery order types, and item categories have been copied from the standard type not to affect the standard types in terms of determination.

1. Customer Consignment Fill-up & Subcontracting Outbound Delivery Schedule Lines

The first schedule line to be configured, is the schedule line of the consignment fill-up. Go to the following path Sales and Distribution>Sales>Sales Documents>Schedule Lines>Define Schedule Line Categories or use Tcode VOV6. Take a copy of E0 schedule line. Add the purchase requisition type, item category 3, and account assignment category M. Note, a new item category was created, item category ZKBN, which is a copy from KBN for this scenario to be managed separately.

2. The second schedule line to be configured, is the schedule line that of the outbound delivery to be generated from the subcontracting purchase order. Take a copy of schedule line category LB. Change the movement type from 541 to 30A, thus, enabling the raw materials to be in transit upon issuing the goods. Note, a new item category was created, item category ZLBN, which is a copy from LBN for this scenario to be managed separately.

2. Requirement Class

1. The Requirement class will be responsible from where the stock is to be issued (Sales Order stock for example) and how the valuation of the finished goods will be managed in the scenario. Create a new requirement class using the following path Sales and Distribution>Basic Functions>Availability Check and Transfer of Requirements>Transfer of Requirements>Configure Requirement Classes or use Tcode OVZG. Under account assignment, add account assignment category M.

a. The account assignment category M is chosen as the sales order will not be carrying costs and revenue, meaning that no settlement of costs is needed on the sales order, this is controlled by leaving the consumption posting field blank in Tcode OME9 (Check Account Assignment Categories). Moreover, the special stock indicator should be E to be able to issue the finished goods to customer consignment after the goods receipt on the subcontracting purchase order from the sales order stock.

2. After setting up the requirement class, go to the following path Sales and Distribution>Basic Functions>Account Assignment/Costing>Transfer of Requirements>Maintain Requirement Classes for Costing/Account Assignment to set up how the costs are to be managed in the customer consignment fill-up. First, is the account assignment category, which is M, assigned in the requirement class. Second, is the valuation M. This indicator is used to indicate that the valuation of the materials in sales order will be valued differently from the standard valuation price of the material manufactured at the company. For the costing ID, add B which means that the sales order will be automatically costed and marked upon saving the sales order. Costing method 1 is used as the costing type used in this scenario is product cost. Meaning that the costs of the finished goods will be brought automatically based on the material's BOM, Routes, and Supplier Info record. Add costing variant PPC4, more explanation on this costing variant will be discussed in the upcoming section. Finally, add condition type line items EK01, as this condition will be the only condition type used in consignment fill-up pricing procedure to be populated from the total value of the cost estimate.

3. Create a new requirement type and assign it to the requirement class from the previous point by going to Sales and Distribution>Basic Functions>Availability Check and Transfer of Requirements>Transfer of Requirements>Configure Requirement Types or use Tcode OVZH.

4. Finally, go to the following path Sales and Distribution>Basic Functions>Availability Check and Transfer of Requirements>Transfer of Requirements>Determine Requirement Type by Item Category and MRP Type or use Tcode OVZI to assign the requirement type to the item category assigned consignment fill-up order type. The 1 under source requirement type means that the requirement class will be determined from item category and not from the material master.

3. Sales Order Cost Estimate

1. The sales order cost estimate is maintained under the following path Controlling>Product Cost Controlling>Cost Object Controlling>Product Cost by Sales Order> Preliminary Costing and Order BOM Costing> Product Costing for Sales Order Items/Order BOMs>Costing Variants for Product Costing>Check Costing Variants for Product Costing or use Tcode OKY9. Using the sales order cost estimate you can control multiple things, including the determination of the BOM (raw materials that make up the finished goods) and routing (Activities such as labour cost per hour) in the sales order cost estimate. To further view the determination of BOM and routing, press on Qty Struct. Control.

a. Press on BOM Application under BOM to view the BOM determination or Routing Selection under Routing to view the routing determination.

b. BOM Selection based on priority, usage, and minimum BOM requirement that is needed in order for BOM to be derived in the sales order cost estimate.

c. Route usage based on priority, task list, plan usage, and status of the routing to be derived in the cost estimate based on the below determination.

2. Note that you need to assign the primary cost elements to the cost component structure, such as for materials consumption and third party maintained in the material account determination (OBYC). This is done in the following path Controlling>Product Cost Controlling>Cost Object Controlling>Product Cost by Sales Order> Preliminary Costing and Order BOM Costing> Product Costing for Sales Order Items/Order BOMs>Define Cost Components or use Tcode OKTZ.

a. Choose the active version of cost component structure.

b. Choose the desired cost component attribute (e.g. Direct Material or Third Party).

c. Assign the cost component to cost element G/L interval that is maintained in your chart of accounts.

4. Customer Consignment Fill-up Pricing Procedure

Below is the pricing schema for the consignment fill-up. Condition EK01 is the same condition type added in the requirement class.

5. Subcontracting Delivery

1. Make a copy from delivery type LB in 0VLK (Define Delivery Types) then go to Materials Management> Purchasing>Purchase Order>Set Up Subcontract Order or use Tcode OMGM and assign the newly created delivery type, ZLB delivery type, to the supplying plant. The supplying plant is then to be added to the supplier's customer role in business partner, as this is how the determination of the delivery type will be derived.

2. To use the schedule line maintained in point 2 in section Customer Consignment Fill-up & Subcontracting Outbound Delivery Schedule lines, the determination of the delivery item category needs to be maintained first. Go to Logistics Execution> Shipping>Deliveries>Define Item Category Determination in Deliveries or use Tcode 0184. In the determination, make sure that the usage used is V as it relates to purchase order. Assignment of item category to schedule line can be then maintained through Tcode VOV5 (Assign Schedule Line Categories).

6. Route Determination for Outbound Delivery - Raw Materials

1. First, maintain transportation relevance for the delivery type of the subcontracting, delivery type ZLB that is mentioned in the previous section, under Logistics Execution>Transportation>Shipments>Shipments>Maintain Transportation Relevance or use Tcode V_TVLK_TR.

2. Second, maintain transportation relevance for the delivery item category type of the subcontracting, delivery type ZLB that is mentioned in the previous section, under Logistics Execution>Transportation>Shipments>Shipments>Maintain Transportation Relevance or use Tcode V_TVLP_TL.

3. Third, maintain route transportation relevance under Logistics Execution>Transportation>Shipments>Shipments>Maintain Transportation Relevance use Tcode V_TVRO.

4. Finally, the route is determined based on the Route Determination with Weight Group (Delivery). Go to Logistics Execution>Transportation>Basic Transportation Functions>Routes>Route Determination>Maintain Route Determination or use Tcode 0VRF. Select the desired departure and receiver zone in step 1 and go to Route Determination with Weight Group (Delivery) step 2, that is because the route here will be determined in delivery and not in order (sales order).

5. Add the route determination as below. Make sure that the weight group to be chosen is 9999. The weight group is determined based on the total weight of the outbound delivery.

a. The weight group is maintained under Route Determination with Weight Group (Delivery). Go to Logistics Execution>Transportation>Basic Transportation Functions>Routes>Route Determination>Define Weight Groups. Here the standard weight group 9999 is maintained.

b. After maintaining the weight group, the standard is designed that the total weight is a large dummy weight as seen below.

7. Goods Issue on Shipment Completion

1. For the sake of this scenario, upon pressing shipment completion in the shipment document (goods in the delivery document to be issued from the company), the following configuration was implemented. Go to the following path Logistics Execution>Transportation>Shipments>Define and Assign Activity Profiles or use Tcode 0VTL. Add Z variant then press on maintain.

2. Select Carry out goods issue posting during save then save the variant.

8. Split Valuation

1. The split valuation is used in this scenario as to split the valuation price cost of the finished product manufactured at company site and the finished product manufactured at subcontractor site. First, you need to activate the split valuation using the following path Materials Management>Valuation and Account Assignment>Split Valuation>Activate Split Valuation or use Tcode OMW0.

2. Next, go to Materials Management>Valuation and Account Assignment>Split Valuation>Configure Split Valuation or use Tcode OMWC. Choose Global Types.

3. Maintain the following two valuation types. Valuation type ZEXTER-SC represents the valuation price of the subcontractor and ZINTER-FG represents the valuation price of the internal manufactured goods. For both EXT.POs and Int.POs, three options are available which are:

  • if the valuation type can't be used for external/internal (STO) purchase orders, which is defined as 0.
  • if the valuation type can be used for external/internal (STO) purchase orders but with warning, which is defined as 1.
  • if the valuation type is allowed be used for external/internal (STO) purchase orders, which is defined as 2.

Finally, the account category reference represents a group for the valuation class assigned to the material master.

a. Press on Account Cat. Ref. to view the assigned valuation classes assigned to the Account Category Reference.

b. To edit the below assignment, go to the following path Materials Management>Valuation and Account Assignment>Account Determination>Account Determination Without Wizard>Define Valuation Classes or use Tcode OMSK and press on Valuation Class

4. Next step is to define the valuation category. Press on Global Categories.

5. The global category is used to define which valuation types can be defaulted when processing external or internal procurement. Both flags, external procurement (ExP) and Inhouse production (Inh), are used for not allowing the valuation type to be overtyped in the procurement process. Press on Types->Cat.

6. Activate both newly created valuation types for the Z valuation category.

7. Final step is to activate the valuation category on plant level. Press on Local Definitions.

8. Select the plant and press on Cats.->OU.

9. Select the valuation category and press on activate.

9. Stock Determination

1. The stock determination is used in this scenario and that is because before any goods issue of outbound delivery for materials that are relevant for split valuation, the valuation type must be entered at either sales order or outbound delivery. This process can be automated when the outbound delivery is subjected to goods issue for non-special stock, meaning it can be automated for normal sales and not sales from consignment stock for example. Other than that, it needs to be added manually. Go to the following path Materials Management>Inventory Management and Physical Inventory>Stock Determination>Define Strategies for Stock Determination or use Tcode OSPX. First, create stock determination group as seen below.

2. Create stock determination rule.

3. Go stock determination Header Table and maintain the combination between the plant to be used for this scenario, the created stock determination group, and the stock determination rule.

a. Double click on the previously created stock determination header table and maintain the below settings.

4. Go to the item level of the stock determination header of the previous step and maintain the below. The F under the special stock indicator column represents the unrestricted stock of the company. It is mandatory to add priority, add 1. Finally, add the valuation type of the internal manufactured goods (created in the split valuation section point 3).

5. Finally, go to Materials Management>Inventory Management and Physical Inventory>Stock Determination>Assign Stock Determination Rule in the Applications>Delivery to assign the created stock determination rule for the delivery item category of the normal sales or consignment fill-up other than the consignment fill-up order used in this scenario.

entry.title: Outbound process with SAP EWM: POSC, WOCR & Wave Management
entry.content:
Outbound logistics is a critical component of supply chain management, ensuring products are delivered to customers efficiently and accurately. In #SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), advanced tools like Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC), Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR), and Wave Management enable seamless execution of outbound processes. This blog provides an overview of a configured scenario to manage a complex outbound process, highlighting each component's role. 

Scenario Overview:

1. Customer Orders and Sales Order Creation:

  • Customer orders are received and processed in SAP.
  • A Sales Order is created in SAP S/4HANA based on customer requirements.
  • An Outbound Delivery is generated against the Sales Order in S/4HANA and distributed to SAP EWM as an Outbound Delivery Order (ODO).

2. Wave Management:

  • The actual EWM process begins upon the creation of the ODO.
  • In this scenario, Waves are created Automatically. A Wave groups multiple Outbound Delivery Orders
    based on specific criteria. For this case, the grouping condition is the Customer.
  • A Wave consolidates all ODOs associated with a single customer, facilitating streamlined operations.

3. Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR):

  • WOCR is used to group Warehouse Tasks into Warehouse Orders based on predefined filters and limits.
  • It also supports the automatic creation of Pick Handling Units (Pick HUs) via packing profiles.
  • In this scenario, three WOCRs are configured for different resources:
    • Human Resources
    • Trolley
    • Forklift

4. Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC):

  • POSC defines the sequential steps required to execute the outbound process.
  • In this scenario, the process includes:
  1. Picking: Pick Handling Units (HUs) are created during picking and moved to the packing work center.
  2. Packing: Products are packed by warehouse associates at the work center. Upon confirmation, an automatic Warehouse Task is created to move products to the Goods Issue (GI) staging area.
  3. Loading: Tasks are generated and confirmed to load products onto the delivery vehicle.
  4. Goods Issue: Final Goods Issue is performed, generating an Outbound Delivery Document in the system.

Upon Goods Issue, Post Processing Framework (PPF) actions are triggered to send the required information back to S/4HANA.

Configuration

This configuration focuses on the key steps, intentionally omitting basic configurations for the sake of brevity. It encompasses three critical components: Wave Management, WOCR, and POSC setup.

I.  Wave Management

As previously mentioned, this configuration involves auto wave creation using PPF Actions and PPF.

1. Maintain Wave Type
2. Maintain Wave Categories
3. Set Automatic Wave Generation for Warehouse Process Type
3.1 PPF Configuration

T.Code: SPPFCADM

Application

/SCDL/DELIVERY

Action Profile

/SCWM/PDO  01

Action Definition

/SCWM/PDO_01_WAVE

Method

/SCWM/WHR_WAVE_ASSIGN_NEW

Make sure Action Definition listed above active for AUTO Wave Generation.

3.2  Outbound Delivery Process Document Types
4. Define Condition Table
5. Define Access Sequence
6. Define Condition Types
7. Define Determination Procedure
8. Assign Procedure to Document type

9. Define Condition Maintenance Group
10. Maintain Wave Template

 
11. Maintain Wave Condition Record

II. Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR)

In the context of WOCR, specific requirements have been defined based on the handling capacities of different resources:

  • WOC1: This rule applies to human resources where the load restriction is a maximum of 2 kilograms, suitable for items that can be carried manually by hand.
  • WOC2: This rule is designated for trolley-based operations, allowing a load capacity of up to 30 kilograms, making it ideal for medium-sized items that require mechanical assistance for movement.
  • WOC3: This rule is intended for heavy-duty equipment capable of managing loads up to 500 kilograms, suitable for large or bulky items that demand specialized handling equipment.

For detailed information and additional context regarding these requirements, please refer to the introduction section provided earlier. The basic configurations, such as number ranges for Warehouse Orders and sorting rules like "PIPA - Pick Path," have already been set up in the system. Let’s focus on the key steps involved in configuring WOCR.

1. Define Filters for warehouse order creation rule

2. Define Limits values for the size of a warehouse order

3. Define packing profile for Warehouse order Creation

3.1 Packaging Specification

Packaging specifications are a prerequisite for the packing profile. When creating a Pick HU, the system uses these specifications to determine the appropriate packaging material.

3.2. Assign Bins to Activity Area

Virtual bins are created for each activity area in advance and assigned accordingly. This step  is crucial for the creation of Pick HUs.

3.3 Warehouse process type for pick HU

3.4 Assign WPT to warehouse as WPT HU as Res

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

4. Define Creation Rule for warehouse Orders

5. Define Search Sequence of creation rules for activity areas

III. Process- Oriented Storage Control (POSC)

The POSC workflow involves the following steps: Upon the release of a wave, picking warehouse tasks are automatically generated, and pick HUs are simultaneously created. These pick HUs are then moved to the designated packing work center, where items are prepared for further processing. Following packing, the items are staged appropriately and subsequently loaded for shipment.

The Warehouse Process Type (WPT) "ZPIC" has already been configured in the system, and corresponding stock removal strategies have been established in alignment with the ZPIC WPT. These configurations ensure that stock removal processes are executed efficiently and adhere to the specific requirements associated with the defined WPT.

1. External Storage process

2. Define Storage process

3. Assign Storage process step

4. Process Oriented Storage Control

Assign storage process steps to their designated locations, such as the staging step to the storage type and the packing step to the corresponding work center.

IV. Work Center

The packing process requires a dedicated work center, for which the layout "VPMX" has been utilized. The standard storage type designated for the packing work center is 8030, and a corresponding bin has been created within the 8030-storage type to facilitate smooth packing operations.

1. Define Work Center

2. Assign Storage bin to Work Center

Test case:

Let’s consider a scenario where orders are received from customers. For instance, two customers place orders for three different products separately. The business requirement is to group these orders based on customers, which is referred to as a wave.

Once the wave is created, the WOCR comes into play. The configuration is set to be released immediately, warehouse tasks are automatically generated and grouped into warehouse orders based on the available resources. In this configuration, resources are categorized as Human, Trolley, and Forklift for picking operations. Additionally, Pick HUs are created automatically during this process.

Warehouse associates perform these tasks and confirm them using RF devices. The workflow then proceeds to move the picked products to the packing area, followed by staging and loading, and finally to the Goods Issue. Each subsequent task is created automatically once the previous task is confirmed which will be executed by the POSC. For simplicity, this blog demonstrates all processes using the Monitor instead of RF. In real-world scenarios, these processes are typically executed via RF in real time.

1. Sales Order Creation (VA01)

10 Sales Orders are created with two different customers for 3 products as shown in the table below. 24-2550(5Q) represents 24 is the Customer, 2550 is the products and 5Q in parenthesis is Quantity.

Sales Order

Customer-Products

Sales Order

Customer- Products

6127

24-2550(5Q)

6126

27-2565(2Q)

6132

24-2551(4Q)

6124

27-2550(30Q)

6133

24-2565(1Q)

6125

27-2551(2Q)

6130

24-2551(10Q)

6134

27-2565(1Q)

6128

24-2550(1Q)

6129

27-2550(2Q)

2. Outbound delivery creation in S/4 (VL01N)

Against Sales Order Created in the S/4, Inbound deliveries will be created, those are listed in the table below.

Sales Order

Inbound Delivery

Sales Order

Inbound Delivery

6124

80005863

6129

80005868

6125

80005864

6130

80005869

6126

80005865

6132

80005870

6127

80005866

6133

80005871

6128

80005867

6134

80005872

All outbound deliveries have been created and successfully distributed to EWM.

3. Outbound Delivery Order in EWM (/N/SCWM/PRDO)

Let’s check whether Outbound deliveries are created and distributed to EWM. Since 10 Outbound deliveries created, searched with advanced search.

Auto Wave Creation:

Waves can be created manually using T-code /N/SCWM/WAVE, but Auto Wave Creation enabled (refer the configuration step I. 3). This process starts with the WPT, triggering PPF actions to generate waves using wave templates 101 and 102, based on condition records configured earlier (refer configuration I. 11). The system created two waves: 5039 for Business Partner 148 and 5040 for Business Partner 129. The PPF log (see image) illustrates the wave creation process as per configuration (refer configuration step I. 3.1). Let’s get into the warehouse Monitor for wider visibility.

4. Wave Release (/N/SCWM/MON)

Two waves were created immediately upon delivery distribution, and they were automatically released. The wave status is marked as "R" (Released), and the wave release method is "B" (Immediate Release), as defined in the wave template. The configuration ensures that outbound delivery orders are grouped by the Ship-to Party, with one wave created for each customer. For instance, outbound deliveries for customer 129 were grouped into a single wave, as shown in the image.

The setup ensures that waves are automatically released for picking tasks as soon as they are created. Alternatively, manual wave release is also possible through the Warehouse Monitor or T-code /N/SCWM/WAVE, depending on the configuration in the wave template.

Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR):

Next, let’s explore the steps following wave release by focusing on a single wave to understand how warehouse operations are executed. Once the wave is released, the system immediately refers to the Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR) for creating warehouse orders.

In this example, three WOCRs were configured, and each was triggered based on the defined filters and weight limits. As shown in the image, the system created the following warehouse orders:WOC1: 2 kg, WOC2: Three orders of 5 kg, 5 kg, and 25 kg and WOC3: 150 kg.

These ranges are clearly defined in the WOCR configuration, where the link between the wave and WOCR is established through the wave category specified in the WOCR filters (refer to the configuration step- II.1).

Warehouse Task creation:

After WOCR processing, the focus shifts to the Warehouse Tasks assigned under the WO. These tasks are created to move stock from the picking storage type to the packing work center, as defined in the configuration.

This process follows the POSC configuration. As shown in the image, the WPT is ZPIC, with the Storage Process and External Step both triggered as ZPIC. The POSC is activated via WOCR, as the storage process is linked in the WOCR determination (refer to configuration step II.4). Picking operations are entirely driven by stock removal strategies, which play a crucial role in the creation of warehouse tasks for picking. However, detailed steps for stock removal strategies are omitted in this blog to maintain its brevity. It’s important to note that understanding these strategies is essential for efficient picking processes in the warehouse.

Let’s confirm picking warehouse tasks…

Auto Pick HU:

Upon confirmation of the warehouse tasks, the destination bin automatically changed to "VIRTUAL_BIN1". This behavior is linked to the Pick Handling Unit (Pick HU) configuration. The system is set to automatically create Pick HUs during the picking process, as defined in the WOCR Packing Profile (refer configuration step- II.3).

Based on the packaging specifications configured in the system, the system assigned the appropriate packaging material to each task. As shown in the image, a unique Pick HU number was created for each WT, ensuring proper handling and alignment with the configuration.

Next, let’s verify whether follow-up tasks were created to move the products from the VIRTUAL_BIN1 to the Packing Work Center for packing. To identify these subsequent tasks,  filter for Open WT with the WPT set to 3070, as specified in the POSC configuration (refer to configuration step III.4).

Subsequent tasks are created based on the external step "ZPAC" with WPT 3070, as configured in the POSC. Confirming these tasks moves the products to the Packing Work Center, which is linked to storage type 8030 and an assigned storage bin (refer to configuration step IV).

After confirming these tasks, no further tasks are generated immediately, as the system awaits a completion update from the work center. Once the system receives this update, it automatically creates the next set of tasks to move the products out of the work center. To update the completion status, the transaction code /N/SCWM/PACK is used.

Packing Work Center:

The "PACK" work center is configured as the designated Packing Work Center using the relevant T-code. In this interface, SAP provides a variety of features, including creating packages, repacking, and other functionalities, to pack products according to customer requirements.

Staging:

Upon completing the process step in the Packing Work Center, the system generates additional tasks with the external step "ZSTG", as illustrated in the image below.

These tasks are created to move the products from the Packing Work Center to the Goods Issue (GI) Staging Area. Let’s proceed to confirm these tasks as well.

Loading:

Once these tasks are confirmed, another set of tasks is generated to move the products from the Staging Area to the Door, completing the loading process. This marks the conclusion of the POSC workflow.

Let’s repeat the same process for the other wave, although the detailed steps are omitted in this blog for brevity. As mentioned earlier, in real-world scenarios, these tasks are not executed in the Monitor but are performed using RF devices. Finally, let’s check the status of the outbound deliveries in the PRDO transaction.

Goods Issue:

As shown in the image, the status of all outbound deliveries has been updated to Picking Completed, Packing Completed, and Loading Completed, indicating successful execution of the POSC process. By selecting Goods Issue, the system completes the goods issue for these outbound deliveries. Once the goods issue is completed, an outbound delivery document is created, triggering PPF actions to transfer this information to the ERP system.

Outbound delivery Document:

Outbound delivery Document created and PPF actions triggered to pass information to the ERP system as expected.

Let’s check whether the information passed to S/4..

T.code : VL03N-  S/4 Display Outbound Delivery

To illustrate the process,  verifying and displaying one of the outbound deliveries in this blog.

The Goods Issue information has been successfully updated in S/4HANA, as indicated by the Goods Movement Status marked as "C" (Completed). The system also triggered the Movement Type 601 in S/4, confirming the successful transfer of goods.

Conclusion

This blog explored the seamless execution of warehouse processes using Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC) in SAP EWM. Starting with Auto Wave Creation & Release and Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR), demonstrated how tasks are efficiently grouped and executed. The flow covered picking, packing, and goods movement from the Picking Storage Type to the Packing Work Center, Goods Issue Staging Area, and finally to the Door for loading.

Key configurations, such as Pick HUs and task automation, were highlighted, along with the integration of EWM and S/4HANA, where updates like Goods Movement Status and Movement Type 601 ensure real-time synchronization. While RF execution and repetitive steps were omitted for brevity, this blog showcased SAP EWM’s ability to optimize warehouse operations efficiently.

entry.title: SAP CAP Lessons Learned: Hybrid Development with On-Premise SAP System
entry.content:
Good day fellow CAPpers,

Today I'll share one of the lessons I've learned working with CAP, something that may not be clear in the current tutorials or documentation. The Cloud Connector has particularity that it doesn't accept connections from anywhere. For security reasons, it only accepts connections, requests, from authorized hosts, such as SAP BTP. This means that your application may work when running on SAP BTP, but not if you're trying to run it locally on a hybrid development. The solution I found out was to route these calls through BTP via an SSH tunnel.

After much struggling, I have found out that there actually is documentation on capire on this topic, but it isn't quite clear. The documentation on SAP Cloud SDK is better, but I'll leave here a step-by-step tutorial specific for CAP.

 

Prerequisites

You obviously need to already have set up the Cloud Connector and the SAP BTP communication with your backend system, be it an ECC or a S/4HANA on-premise.

If you have already made your app work when running on SAP BTP, you know that you need to have bound your application to a Connectivity service in order that the Destination works properly.

Finally, you need to have already done the bindings locally for the Hybrid Development.

  • cds bind --to my-app-auth
  • cds bind --to my-app-destination-service
  • cds bind --to my-app-connectivity

 

Local Setup

In your local environment, you'll have to edit your .cdsrc-private.json to overwrite the connectivity.binding.credentials.onpremise_proxy_host property to point to localhost :

 

{
  "requires": {
    "[hybrid]": {
      "connectivity": {
        "binding": {
          "type": "cf",
          "apiEndpoint": "https://api.cf.us10.hana.ondemand.com",
          "org": "org-name",
          "space": "DEV",
          "instance": "my-app-connectivity",
          "key": "my-app-connectivity-key",
          "resolved": false,
          "credentials": {
            "onpremise_proxy_host": "localhost"
          }
        },
        "kind": "connectivity",
        "vcap": {
          "name": "connectivity"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

 

Note: update apiEndpoint, org, space, instance, key according to your own configurations.

On the actual service, this property points to the Cloud Connector's host. We'll overwrite it to point to our local computer.

Next, you'll do a one-time setup on your deployed app. It will be used as a proxy for your requests. You have to enable the SSH on it and restart for the changes to take effect:

  • cf enable-ssh my-app-srv
  • cf restart my-app-srv

The setup is complete.

 

Running the app on hybrid mode

Now, every time you want to run your CAP app on hybrid mode, first you'll need to open a separate terminal and enable the ssh forwarding and leave it running:

  • cf ssh my-app-srv -L 20003:10.0.4.5:20003

On a different terminal, you can run your CAP app:

  • cds watch --profile hybrid

 

Note: the hosts and port may change according to your configurations:

  • cf ssh MY-APP -L LOCAL-HOST:LOCAL-PORT:REMOTE-HOST:REMOTE-HOST-PORT

 

Acknowledgements

  • @pieterjanssens  on this GitHub issue that started the discussion and documentation update.
  • The SAP Cloud SDK documentation.
  • @joachimvanpraet for the sap-cf-proxy, which was not used but helped me understand the issue.

 

 

May this post help you on your troubleshooting. Reply if you find any issues so I, and the community, can help.

Lucas Magrini Rigo

entry.title: Hybrid Partner Add-ons for on-premise & BTP ABAP SaaS
entry.content:
CONTAX has provided SAP certified add-ons since the release of ECC 6.0. Maintaining backwards compatibility used to mean publishing each software component from an on-prem SAP landscape which running the min version required to install an said Partner add-on. With the advent of SAP BTP ABAP and clean-core, delivering partner products to both on-premise and cloud systems with various ABAP language versions has become more complex. 

Our objective is to deliver clean core Partner products that are installable and available via BTP SaaS while maintaining a single ABAP codebase. Since I did not come accross any guides on how to set up a landscape for both on-premise and cloud scenerios, I will share my approach with the community.

First let's look at SAPs minimum recommeded landscape for publishing Partner Products on the BTP ABAP Environment. We're looking at at least a 4 system landscape.

Source: ~27' How to build multitenancy SaaS applications

 

Next, consider deployment options from the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. Devtoberfest 2024 recently announced that SAP BTP ABAP supports multitenancy SaaS & has planned support for Installable Products coming 2025! Easy-peasy lemon squeezy 🍋, right? 

Source: Building Partner Products on SAP BTP ABAP Environment

Not quite 🤔... Installable products published via BTP ABAP can only be installed on another SAP BTP ABAP system. Since there is no ERP backend (S4 Core) on BTP ABAP, enterprise customers who run S/4 HANA Cloud private Edition or S/4 HANA on-premise will not be able to install a product published from BTP ABAP regardless of their release.

We have determined a codebase developed in The BTP ABAP environment is not compatible with other SAP systems. Therefore, we will need design our Hybrid landscape to initially develop and publish the add-on from an on-prem system then later move the codebase into our BTP ABAP envrionment landscape to enable SaaS subscriptions. Much like the good ol' days of ECC 6.0, a minimum release level must be chosen. The minimum version needs to at least run syntax version ABAP Cloud for forward compatibility. S/4 HANA 2022 and up with Add-on Assembly Kit (AAK) installed for publishing add-ons is required for a hybrid landscape.

Source: ABAP Cloud FAQs

A primary goal is to share as much application code as possible between on-premise and SaaS scenerios to reduce overhead and the risk of bugs scenarios. To accomplish this, we need to interact with all reference data through only SAP released APIs. This makes maintaining our codebase more simple, and adheres to SAPs clean core concept. Therefore, another important consideration for determining the released API versions available on Business Accelerator Hub APIs that will be used by your Partner Product. 

For our use case, we selected an S/4 HANA 2023 for our on-premise landscape. Shown below, 2020 FPS02 is minimum release you would need to interface with the Business Partner (A2X) API. SAP recommends using communication targets for ABAP API usage. For more detail see Enable HTTP Communication in your ABAP code.

A very important note BTP ABAP provides only the technology layer, but not the application layer of S/4HANA (like Financials and delivery data). This should be enfored through package 'Use Accesses'. For example, a use access to reference SAP objects in the SAP_BASIS component can safely be moved from S/4 HANA to SAP BTP ABAP but a reference to S4CORE is not permitted.

 

Great! We've determined our on-premise development and consolidation (w/ AAK) landscape will use S/4 HANA 2023.

I'd recommend going through the AAK process in your 2023 Consolidation system to publish installables & supports packs files (.SAR) that have passed all code and use access checks. These Sar files should installed into a stand-alone On-Premise Test system. ABAPGit can be used to the codebase from your on-prem Test system to our BTP ABAP landscape. This is approach, our installables & support packs should allign with the release schedule of our BTP ABAP environment. 

 

Let's reconsider our BTP ABAP Environment landscape. The DEV and TST system from the minimum landscape can be dropped since we will be using on-premise S/4 HANA 2023 Dev and S/4 HANA 2023 Consolidation systems. 

Resulting in a hybrid partner landscape with support for both current and future on-premise SAP releases  as well as BTP ABAP SaaS arcitecture.

hybrid partner add-on Landscape

 

If you've read this far, Thanks and please drop a comment. How are you designing your SAP Partner Product landscape?
